opts.rat: Mechanical issue worth checking - is the drain leveled, transducer zeroed, drainage flowing, no clots/debris? Quick bedside check that can solve the problem in seconds if found.

opts.text: Seizure activity (sub-clinical) raising ICP via increased metabolic demand
opts.rat: Possible especially with paralytic; continuous EEG monitoring helps. Less likely than the structural/dynamic causes above but worth considering if other interventions fail.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30 minutes for ICP 32 with Cushing's triad emerging.

opts.text: Drain CSF via the EVD per neurosurgery parameters (e.g., 5-10 mL or open to drain at set pressure for set duration)
opts.rat: FASTEST way to drop ICP. Removes one component of the Monro-Kellie volume equation. Works within seconds. Always check transducer level (tragus or foramen of Monro) and drainage chamber height before use.

opts.text: Administer 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL bolus (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg if normotensive) as ordered by neurosurgery
opts.rat: Hyperosmolar therapy is the mainstay of medical ICP reduction. Effects within 15-30 min and last hours. 3% saline preferred in trauma to preserve volume; mannitol works faster but diureses. Class I recommendation.

opts.text: Optimize sedation (bolus propofol or fentanyl for ICP spikes), ensure HOB 30 degrees with head midline and neck free of obstruction, verify ETT ties not compressing jugular veins
opts.rat: Free interventions that rapidly reduce ICP. Sedation reduces metabolic demand and patient-vent dyssynchrony. Positioning optimizes venous drainage. Check anything that could obstruct cerebral venous return.

opts.text: Initiate IV bolus of 1 L 0.45% NS to optimize cerebral perfusion
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Hypotonic fluids worsen cerebral edema by shifting water INTO brain. Always use isotonic (0.9% NS) or hypertonic fluids in TBI - never hypotonic. NCLEX favorite trap.

opts.text: Hyperventilate aggressively to PaCO2 of 25 mmHg and maintain
opts.rat: PROLONGED hyperventilation causes ischemia. Brief rescue hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-35 is acceptable as bridge to definitive therapy in impending herniation, but never to 25 and never sustained.

opts.text: Administer dexamethasone 10 mg IV for the cerebral edema
opts.rat: Steroids INCREASE mortality in TBI. Use only for tumor/abscess vasogenic edema. Major NCLEX trap.

opts.text: Suction the ETT vigorously every 15 minutes to clear secretions
opts.rat: Suctioning RAISES ICP - perform only when clinically indicated, pre-oxygenate, limit duration to <10 sec, and consider lidocaine pre-treatment in TBI patients to blunt the response.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Verify HOB at 30 degrees, head midline, neck untwisted, no ETT tie compression; bolus propofol/fentanyl for sedation; check EVD level and patency, drain CSF per orders
opts.rat: FIRST - immediate, reversible interventions that take seconds. Position and venous outflow optimization plus CSF drainage can drop ICP significantly without medication. Always verify the basics before escalating.

opts.text: Notify neurosurgeon and intensivist of ICP trend and emerging Cushing's triad; verify orders for hyperosmolar therapy
opts.rat: SECOND - rapid escalation to provider for orders. Cushing's triad is an emergency requiring senior decision-making. Don't wait to communicate.

opts.text: Administer 3% hypertonic saline 250 mL bolus over 15 min (or mannitol per orders); check serum sodium and osmolality before and after
opts.rat: THIRD - definitive medical ICP reduction. Pre/post labs ensure safe targeting. Often given concurrently with the immediate maneuvers above.

opts.text: Coordinate stat repeat CT head to evaluate for expanding hemorrhage or new mass effect requiring surgery
opts.rat: FOURTH - imaging guides whether medical management is sufficient or whether OR is needed. Transport itself raises ICP, so optimize patient first.

opts.text: Update mother (healthcare proxy) on the change in condition, what is being done, and what may be needed next; offer chaplaincy/social work; prepare her for possible escalation conversations
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - critical communication that preserves trust and enables informed decisions later. The proxy needs to understand the seriousness so she can make decisions if the trajectory worsens.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After CSF drainage, hyperosmolar therapy, and sedation optimization, classify each finding over the next 6 hours.

opts.text: ICP 14 mmHg, CPP 72 mmHg, sustained over 2 hours of monitoring
opts.rat: ICP back within target (<22) and CPP within optimal range (60-70). The combination of EVD drainage, hyperosmolar therapy, and sedation optimization succeeded. Continue current measures and re-evaluate next 24-72 hour window.

opts.text: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, briskly reactive; sedation hold reveals withdrawal to noxious stimulation in all 4 extremities (improvement from posturing)
opts.rat: Pupillary recovery and improved motor response on sedation hold are the strongest neurologic improvement markers. Sedation 'breaks' once daily allow exam without obscuring the picture.

opts.text: Serum Na 152 mEq/L, osmolality 312 mOsm/kg, urine output 80 mL/hr
opts.rat: Therapeutic hyperosmolar state achieved (target 145-155 / 300-320). Adequate UO indicates volume status preserved. Continue 3% saline to maintain target and monitor q6h labs.

opts.text: Bilateral pupil dilation to 6 mm, fixed and unreactive, with sustained ICP at 45 mmHg despite repeat hyperosmolar therapy
opts.rat: HERNIATION - immediate emergency. Brief rescue hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-35, repeat hyperosmolar therapy, prepare for emergent decompressive craniectomy if surgical candidate, escalate goals-of-care discussion with family. Devastating finding.

opts.text: Massive urine output (>500 mL/hr) with serum Na rising to 158 and dropping urine specific gravity
opts.rat: Diabetes insipidus - common in severe TBI from posterior pituitary injury. Lose enormous volumes of dilute urine; can cause hypovolemic shock and worsen cerebral perfusion. Treat with desmopressin (DDAVP) and careful fluid replacement.

opts.text: New non-displaced fever to 38.6°C; cultures pending; WBC 14.8
opts.rat: Possible early VAP, central-line infection, or central fever from hypothalamic injury - all of which raise ICP via increased metabolic demand. Workup, cooling measures, and acetaminophen. Maintaining normothermia reduces ICP and improves outcomes.

opts.text: Glucose 152, BP 138/76, lactate 1.2 mmol/L, urine output 70 mL/hr
opts.rat: Stable systemic parameters - not improving but not worsening. Stable physiology supports the brain-directed therapy. Continue tight glucose control (target 140-180), normotension, and avoidance of secondary insults.

opts.text: Mother and family verbalize understanding of injury severity, treatment plan, and prognostic uncertainty; engaged with chaplaincy; bringing extended family to bedside
opts.rat: Family processing and engagement is a meaningful psychosocial outcome - not in the trajectory of the brain injury, but in the quality of the experience for everyone involved. Critical care nursing includes the family as patient.

title: Major Burn Injury with Inhalation Injury
subtitle: 34 y/o male, house fire, 38% TBSA partial and full-thickness burns
nursesNote: 1545 - 34 y/o male, weight 80 kg, brought to burn center via helicopter from a residential house fire (rescued by firefighters from upstairs bedroom). Estimated 25-30 min in enclosed smoke-filled space. Burns: anterior chest and abdomen (full circumference of trunk anteriorly), bilateral anterior arms full circumference, anterior thighs - estimated 38% TBSA mixed second-degree (partial-thickness) and third-degree (full-thickness). Singed nasal hair, soot in oropharynx, hoarse voice, carbonaceous sputum, audible stridor on arrival. Field intubated by flight team for impending airway compromise; 7.5 ETT secured at 22 cm. Two large-bore peripheral IVs in unburned skin; arterial line placed in burn center ED. Foley placed - initial UO 18 mL dark amber. Carboxyhemoglobin 22% on co-oximetry; ABG drawn. Tetanus updated. Pain management started with IV fentanyl. Family notified, en route. Burn surgeon at bedside; OR prepped for possible escharotomy.

patient.name: D. Whitehorse
patient.admitDate: Today, 1545 - direct admit to burn ICU

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish the most immediately life-threatening complications in this burn patient.

opts.text: Singed nasal hair, soot in oropharynx, hoarse voice, carbonaceous sputum, stridor before intubation - all signs of inhalation injury and impending airway obstruction
opts.rat: Inhalation injury kills more burn patients than the burns themselves. Upper airway swelling progresses rapidly over hours - intubation must happen EARLY, before the airway closes. The field team made the right call. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 467.

opts.text: Carboxyhemoglobin 22% with metabolic acidosis (pH 7.28, lactate 5.4) - signs of carbon monoxide poisoning and tissue hypoxia despite normal SpO2
opts.rat: CO has 250x the affinity for hemoglobin vs O2; pulse oximetry reads CO-Hgb as oxygen, giving FALSE NORMAL SpO2 readings. COHb >20% is severe; >40% is life-threatening. Treatment: 100% oxygen (reduces half-life from 4 hr to 90 min) or hyperbaric oxygen for severe cases.

opts.text: Circumferential full-thickness burns of trunk (anterior) and bilateral arms - risk of compartment syndrome and respiratory restriction from chest eschar
opts.rat: Circumferential eschar acts like a tourniquet - cutting off circulation in extremities (limb ischemia) or restricting chest expansion (preventing ventilation). Escharotomy (longitudinal incisions through eschar) within 24-48 hr is often required. Specifically tested NCLEX concept.

opts.text: Heart rate 128-136 with BP trending down (108/68 -> 92/58)
opts.rat: Reflects hypovolemic shock from burn fluid losses - important but EXPECTED finding in a major burn during the resuscitation phase. Treated by appropriate Parkland fluid resuscitation, not a separate complication.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 16.8 g/dL
opts.rat: Hemoconcentration from massive plasma loss into burn tissue - reflects volume depletion. Will normalize with resuscitation. Not a primary complication.

opts.text: CK 4,820 with myoglobinuria
opts.rat: Wait - this IS a serious complication (rhabdomyolysis from deep tissue burns + electrical/crush mechanisms) that risks AKI. Important. But the question asks for the THREE MOST life-threatening, and inhalation/CO/circumferential burns are immediately lethal in hours; rhabdo is a renal threat over hours-to-days.

opts.text: Initial UO 18 mL dark amber
opts.rat: Reflects volume depletion + early myoglobinuria - both expected and tracked. Not in itself the most life-threatening cue.

opts.text: Tetanus updated
opts.rat: Important infection prevention but not a life-threatening cue - it's a treatment decision already made.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Link cues to pathophysiology and the Parkland formula
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: Parkland formula: 4 mL x weight (kg) x %TBSA = total LR for first 24 hr; HALF given in first 8 hours from time of INJURY (not arrival), other half over the next 16 hours; this patient: 4 x 80 x 38 = 12,160 mL in first 24 hr, with ~6,080 mL in the first 8 hours
opts.rat: The most-tested calculation in burn nursing. Time-of-injury, not time-of-arrival, determines the 8-hour clock - so any pre-hospital time eats into the 8-hour window. Lactated Ringer's is the standard fluid (avoids hyperchloremic acidosis of NS).

opts.text: Massive capillary leak in burn injury causes plasma to shift into the interstitium, producing intravascular hypovolemia + tissue edema simultaneously - the patient is 'drowning while being dehydrated'; titrate fluid to UO 0.5 mL/kg/hr (or 1 mL/kg/hr if rhabdomyolysis present)
opts.rat: Burn shock is unique - hypovolemia despite massive fluid administration. UO is the bedside marker of resuscitation adequacy. Higher UO target (1 mL/kg/hr) when myoglobin is present to flush the kidneys and prevent pigment-induced AKI.

opts.text: Pulse oximetry reads carboxyhemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin, giving FALSELY NORMAL SpO2 in CO poisoning; co-oximetry on ABG is required to detect CO; 100% oxygen reduces COHb half-life from 4-5 hours (room air) to 60-90 minutes
opts.rat: Critical pitfall. The pulse ox can read 100% on a dying CO-poisoned patient. Always assume CO toxicity in fire victims; confirm with arterial co-oximetry; treat empirically with 100% FiO2 immediately. NCLEX favorite.

opts.text: Cooling burns with ice or ice water in the first hour reduces tissue damage and should be the first nursing intervention
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Ice causes vasoconstriction and DEEPENS the burn. Cool (not cold) running water for <10 min is appropriate at the scene; once in the hospital, focus on stopping the burning process, not cooling. Hypothermia is a major mortality risk in major burns.

opts.text: Albumin and other colloids should be administered in the first 12 hours as part of standard Parkland resuscitation
opts.rat: Capillary leak makes colloids leak into the interstitium WITH the water in the first 24 hours, worsening edema. Colloids (albumin) are added AFTER 12-24 hours when capillary integrity returns. Use crystalloid (LR) initially.

opts.text: Wound debridement and grafting should be the first surgical intervention after fluid resuscitation begins
opts.rat: Definitive wound care comes AFTER initial resuscitation and ICU stabilization. The first surgery in major burns is typically ESCHAROTOMY for circumferential burns - this is what releases the constriction. Definitive grafting follows in days.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the active threats from MOST life-threatening (1) to LEAST life-threatening (5).

opts.text: Inhalation injury with progressive upper airway edema (airway threat)
opts.rat: Highest priority - airway closes within hours and intubation becomes nearly impossible once edema peaks. Field intubation already performed, but management of the secured airway and lower respiratory injury continues.

opts.text: Burn shock with hypovolemia from massive plasma loss
opts.rat: Without aggressive Parkland resuscitation, mortality from burn shock alone is essentially 100%. The first 24 hours of fluid management is what saves lives in major burns.

opts.text: Carbon monoxide toxicity with tissue hypoxia
opts.rat: Severe at COHb 22%; treated with 100% FiO2. Without recognition and treatment, causes anoxic brain injury and death. Already addressed by intubation with 100% O2.

opts.text: Compartment syndrome from circumferential eschar (chest restriction + limb ischemia)
opts.rat: Develops over hours as edema accumulates beneath rigid eschar. Requires escharotomy. Untreated causes ventilatory failure (chest) or limb loss (extremities).

opts.text: Rhabdomyolysis with pigment-induced AKI
opts.rat: Important but slower-developing complication. Aggressive fluid resuscitation with UO target 1 mL/kg/hr and possible alkalinization addresses it. Not immediately life-threatening compared to airway/shock.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next hour.

opts.text: Initiate Parkland fluid resuscitation: ~6,080 mL LR in first 8 hours from time of injury, with hourly UO target 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr (higher target due to myoglobinuria); titrate up or down based on UO response
opts.rat: The cornerstone of burn ICU care. Pre-hospital fluid counts toward total. Continuous UO monitoring drives titration - too little = under-resuscitated; too much = 'fluid creep' with worse edema. The math should be at the bedside.

opts.text: Maintain 100% FiO2 through the ventilator until COHb is <5% to accelerate CO elimination; consider hyperbaric oxygen consultation if available and patient stable enough
opts.rat: 100% O2 is the antidote to CO poisoning. Reduces COHb half-life from 4 hr to ~90 min. Hyperbaric reduces it further to ~25 min and may benefit patients with COHb >25% or with neurologic findings - but transport risk must be weighed.

opts.text: Coordinate with burn surgeon for emergent escharotomy if peak airway pressures rise (chest eschar restricting ventilation) or distal extremities show ischemia (cool, pulseless, mottled)
opts.rat: Decompression is sometimes life- or limb-saving. Bedside procedure performed at the burn center. Nurses watch for the indicators (rising peak airway pressures, lost distal pulses, mottled extremities) and escalate immediately.

opts.text: Submerge the patient in cool water to halt thermal damage
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Hypothermia is a major killer in burn patients - they can't thermoregulate without skin. Maintain warm room (>30°C/85°F), warmed fluids, warming blankets. Cool water submersion causes precipitous hypothermia and worsens shock.

opts.text: Apply silver sulfadiazine cream to burns immediately on arrival
opts.rat: Silver sulfadiazine is INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS for the wound bed - it's appropriate but NOT time-critical in the first hour. Airway, breathing, circulation come first. Topical care happens after stabilization and initial debridement.

opts.text: Bolus 2 L 0.9% NS rapidly to restore circulating volume
opts.rat: Wrong fluid choice. LACTATED RINGER'S is the standard burn fluid - normal saline causes hyperchloremic acidosis with the volumes needed. The Parkland calculation is the standard, not arbitrary boluses.

opts.text: Restrict pain medication to avoid masking complications
opts.rat: WRONG. Burn pain is severe and undertreatment is a major nursing failure. IV opioids (often fentanyl) titrated for both rest pain and procedural pain are essential. Adequate analgesia does not 'mask' the airway, breathing, or circulatory exams.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Verify ETT placement and ventilator settings (FiO2 100%, PEEP, lung-protective TV); auscultate bilateral breath sounds; ensure continuous SpO2, ETCO2, and cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: FIRST - airway and breathing always come first. ETT was placed in the field, but verification is the receiving nurse's responsibility. Continuous monitoring is the bedside safety net.

opts.text: Calculate and initiate Parkland fluid resuscitation (4 mL x 80 kg x 38% = 12,160 mL LR; ~6,080 in first 8 hr from injury); set hourly UO target; ensure two large-bore IVs (preferably in unburned skin)
opts.rat: SECOND - circulation. Parkland math at the bedside. The 8-hour clock starts at injury, not arrival. Document time-of-injury prominently and recalculate hourly rates if needed.

opts.text: Perform thorough secondary survey: complete burn assessment with Rule of Nines, distal pulse and capillary refill checks (especially circumferential burns), Foley with hourly UO, gastric tube if ileus expected, weights for fluid calculations
opts.rat: THIRD - systematic head-to-toe assessment. Burn % drives all the math; missed burns mean under-resuscitation. Pulse checks identify compartment syndrome before it becomes irreversible.

opts.text: Initiate adequate IV opioid analgesia (often fentanyl); ensure tetanus current; begin warming measures (warm room, warmed IV fluids, warming blanket); prepare for likely escharotomy
opts.rat: FOURTH - supportive interventions. Pain control is humane and physiologically beneficial (reduces catecholamine surge). Hypothermia prevention is critical. Escharotomy readiness is anticipatory.

opts.text: Update family on injury severity, treatment plan, expected ICU course, prognostic uncertainty; coordinate burn social worker, chaplain, and family room access
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - critical communication. Burn families face long, traumatic journeys; early connection with social work and chaplaincy prevents crisis later. Honest, compassionate communication is part of nursing care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 24 hours of Parkland resuscitation, escharotomy of chest, and supportive care, classify each finding.

opts.text: Hourly UO sustained at 60-80 mL/hr; lactate cleared from 5.4 to 1.6 mmol/L; pH normalized to 7.38
opts.rat: Resuscitation success: UO in target range, lactate cleared, acidosis resolved. The Parkland formula worked. Begin tapering fluids and transitioning to maintenance for the second 24 hours per protocol.

opts.text: Carboxyhemoglobin trended from 22% to 3% over 8 hours on 100% FiO2
opts.rat: Successful CO elimination. FiO2 can now be weaned based on PaO2 and SpO2 (now reliable since CO is cleared). Continued monitoring for delayed neurologic sequelae of CO poisoning over coming weeks.

opts.text: Post-escharotomy: peak airway pressures dropped from 38 to 22 cmH2O; chest wall expansion symmetric; bilateral upper extremity pulses palpable and Doppler-confirmed
opts.rat: Escharotomy successfully released the chest restriction and limb compression. Peak airway pressure normalization indicates restored lung compliance.

opts.text: Sudden urine output drop to <10 mL/hr with rising peak airway pressures and tense distended abdomen
opts.rat: ABDOMINAL COMPARTMENT SYNDROME from massive resuscitation - intra-abdominal pressure compresses IVC, kidneys, and diaphragm. Measure bladder pressure; if >20 mmHg, decompressive laparotomy may be needed. 'Fluid creep' complication of over-resuscitation.

opts.text: Persistent dark amber urine with CK rising to 12,400; creatinine climbing 1.4 -> 2.1 -> 2.8 mg/dL
opts.rat: Worsening rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI. Increase fluid rate to achieve UO 1-1.5 mL/kg/hr, consider sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (target pH >6.5), monitor potassium, prepare for possible CRRT if oliguria persists.

opts.text: Fever 38.4°C, WBC 18.4, white plaques visible at burn wound edges 48 hours into admission
opts.rat: Burn wound infection - greatest single cause of mortality after the first 48 hours in major burns. Wound culture, possible biopsy, systemic antibiotics (often vancomycin + antipseudomonal). Topical silver products help but cannot replace systemic treatment if invasive infection.

opts.text: Patient awake, sedation lightened, following commands, communicating non-verbally with family while intubated
opts.rat: Neurologic recovery (no anoxic brain injury from CO), psychosocial engagement with family - meaningful outcomes that go beyond physiology. Family presence and patient orientation reduce ICU delirium.

opts.text: Hemoglobin trending down from 16.8 to 11.2 g/dL with stable hemodynamics
opts.rat: Hemoconcentration resolving as plasma volume restored - this is EXPECTED and reassuring, not bleeding. Reflects successful intravascular volume restoration. Stable hemodynamics confirm no active hemorrhage. Don't transfuse based on hemodilution alone.

title: Spinal Cord Injury with Autonomic Dysreflexia
subtitle: 38 y/o male, T4 SCI from 6 weeks ago, sudden severe headache and HTN crisis
nursesNote: 1410 - 38 y/o male, 6 weeks post-injury T4 complete spinal cord injury (ASIA A) sustained in MVC. On rehabilitation unit working on transfer training and self-cath schedule. Within the past 30 minutes, he developed sudden severe pounding headache, profuse sweating above the level of injury, flushed face/upper chest, and goosebumps. Wife reports he has been unable to self-cath for the past 7 hours due to spasticity-related difficulty. Skin below T4 cool and pale; skin above T4 flushed and diaphoretic. He is anxious, blurred vision, anxious about 'something feeling really wrong.' On the bedside dynamap, BP read 198/118 with HR 52 - significantly higher than his baseline (typically 96/62). His Foley was discontinued 2 weeks ago in favor of intermittent self-cath schedule q4-6h. Last bowel movement 4 days ago per chart. Bedside bladder scan: 740 mL of urine in bladder. Skin assessment shows no breakdown, no obvious noxious stimuli on exam. No recent procedure. Charge nurse and physician notified; rapid intervention beginning.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish autonomic dysreflexia (AD) in this client.

opts.text: Sudden severe HTN (198/118) far above baseline (96/62) - a >20 mmHg SBP rise from baseline in an SCI patient at T6 or above is diagnostic for AD
opts.rat: The defining feature. SCI baselines run low (sympathetic outflow disrupted); a >20 mmHg SBP rise from baseline is the diagnostic threshold per Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine guidelines. Patient is far above this. AD only occurs in injuries at T6 or above (where sympathetic outflow can be triggered below the lesion without supraspinal modulation).

opts.text: Bradycardia (HR 52, dropped from baseline 68) reflecting baroreceptor-mediated parasympathetic response to sudden severe HTN
opts.rat: Classic paradoxical finding. Above the lesion, the body senses high BP via carotid baroreceptors and triggers parasympathetic slowing - the brain CAN'T send the sympathetic signal down past the injury to vasodilate, so the BP keeps climbing. Bradycardia + HTN is a hallmark.

opts.text: Sweating, flushing, goosebumps, and pounding headache ABOVE the level of injury, with cool/pale skin BELOW the level of injury
opts.rat: The split presentation is pathognomonic. Below T4: massive sympathetic discharge from the spinal cord causing vasoconstriction (cool, pale, piloerection). Above T4: parasympathetic-mediated vasodilation trying to lower BP (flushing, sweating). The transition line marks the lesion level.

opts.text: Bladder scan showing 740 mL of urine retained
opts.rat: The TRIGGER, not the cue. Bladder distention is the #1 cause of AD (~85%). It establishes the source but not the diagnosis - the diagnosis is the AD itself (HTN + above-injury symptoms). Critical to recognize and remove.

opts.text: Anxiety and blurred vision
opts.rat: These are SYMPTOMS but nonspecific - many conditions cause both. They don't establish AD; the BP and split-presentation findings do.

opts.text: Last bowel movement 4 days ago
opts.rat: Constipation/fecal impaction is the SECOND most common AD trigger - important to find and resolve - but it's another trigger, not a diagnostic cue.

opts.text: Urinalysis with nitrites and leukocyte esterase
opts.rat: UTI is a common AD trigger and may need to be treated, but it's not what establishes the diagnosis. Bladder retention is the immediate culprit here.

opts.text: Glucose 94 mg/dL
opts.rat: Normal finding - rules out hypoglycemia as cause of symptoms. Not a cue of AD.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Below the SCI level, a noxious stimulus (full bladder, impacted stool, pressure injury, ingrown nail) triggers a massive sympathetic reflex through the intact thoracolumbar sympathetic chain - causing severe vasoconstriction and HTN
opts.rat: Core mechanism. The reflex IS intact below the injury - what's missing is supraspinal modulation. So once triggered, sympathetic outflow goes unchecked. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 1467.

opts.text: Above the level of injury, the brain senses the HTN via carotid baroreceptors and tries to compensate with parasympathetic activation (vagal slowing of the heart) and vasodilation - but it CANNOT send sympathetic-inhibiting signals below the lesion to stop the constriction, so the cycle continues until the trigger is removed
opts.rat: Explains the split presentation and why removing the trigger is the only definitive fix. Antihypertensive medications buy time but cannot fix the source. Specifically tested NCLEX concept.

opts.text: AD is a medical EMERGENCY - sustained severe HTN can cause hemorrhagic stroke, seizures, retinal detachment, MI, and death; SBP >180 demands immediate intervention
opts.rat: Mortality is real. CSCM guidelines recommend immediate action for SBP >150 in adult SCI patients. Stroke and intracranial hemorrhage are documented complications. Family/patient education on AD recognition is a major nursing role to prevent home deaths.

opts.text: Place the patient flat (supine) to maximize venous return and improve cerebral perfusion
opts.rat: BACKWARDS. Sit the patient UP (high Fowler's, with legs dependent) to use orthostatic effect to lower BP. The opposite of nearly every other emergency. NCLEX classic.

opts.text: AD occurs in ALL spinal cord injuries regardless of level
opts.rat: Only at T6 or above (some sources say T7 in chronic injury). Below T6, the splanchnic vascular bed doesn't generate enough vasoconstriction to drive systemic HTN. Lower-level SCI patients can have other autonomic issues but not classic AD.

opts.text: Antihypertensive medications (e.g., nitroglycerin paste, nifedipine) should be the FIRST intervention before any other action
opts.rat: WRONG ORDER. Find and remove the trigger FIRST (bladder, bowel, skin); BP often drops dramatically once the trigger is gone. Antihypertensives are the SECOND line if BP remains dangerously elevated after trigger removal. Standard mnemonic: 'Sit up, find the trigger, treat the trigger, then medicate.'

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the most likely AD triggers in this client from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Bladder distention from 7-hour delay in self-cath (740 mL retained urine)
opts.rat: Bladder distention causes ~85% of AD episodes. Bladder scan confirms massive retention. Highest pre-test probability and quickest to address.

opts.text: Fecal impaction from 4 days without bowel movement
opts.rat: Bowel impaction causes ~10-15% of AD. Important contributor here. Address AFTER the bladder is emptied (clearing one trigger may resolve the AD even if a second trigger persists).

opts.text: Urinary tract infection (cloudy urine, +nitrites, +LE)
opts.rat: UTI is a common AD trigger - bacterial inflammation of the bladder wall is itself noxious. Important to treat but bladder emptying first.

opts.text: Pressure injury or skin breakdown below the level of injury
opts.rat: Common AD trigger but assessment showed no obvious breakdown. Worth a thorough re-inspection (sacrum, heels, ischial tuberosities, areas under braces) once acute episode managed.

opts.text: Tight clothing or restrictive dressing
opts.rat: Possible trigger that's quick to check - any constriction below the lesion can trigger AD. Lower probability than the dominant bladder/bowel issues but worth ruling out.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 5-10 minutes.

opts.text: SIT the patient UP in high Fowler's with legs dependent over the side of the bed if tolerated, and loosen any constrictive clothing or abdominal binder
opts.rat: FIRST action. Uses orthostatic effect to drop BP. Opposite of most emergencies. Loosening clothing addresses possible constrictive trigger and supports venous pooling. Free, immediate, no orders needed.

opts.text: Insert a Foley catheter (or use an indwelling cath if already present, after checking for kinks) and drain the bladder slowly to decompress; if catheter already in place, irrigate and verify patency
opts.rat: SECOND action - addresses the most likely trigger. Use lidocaine jelly to minimize the procedure as a stimulus itself. Drain slowly to avoid hypotension rebound. Bladder is often the only intervention needed.

opts.text: Take BP every 2-5 minutes; if SBP remains >150 after sitting up and bladder drained, administer fast-acting antihypertensive (nitroglycerin paste 1-inch to chest above lesion - easy to remove, OR nifedipine immediate-release per protocol)
opts.rat: Pharmacologic management for persistent HTN. Nitroglycerin paste is preferred because it can be wiped off if BP drops too rapidly. Nifedipine IR is alternative. Avoid IV antihypertensives if possible - they can cause precipitous drops.

opts.text: Place the patient supine and elevate legs to improve cerebral perfusion
opts.rat: OPPOSITE of correct action. Will WORSEN the HTN by increasing venous return. NCLEX favorite trap question.

opts.text: Administer beta-blocker (metoprolol IV) to control the heart rate
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Patient is already bradycardic (HR 52). Beta-blocker would worsen bradycardia, possibly cause arrest, and doesn't address HTN root cause. Contraindicated.

opts.text: Manually disimpact the rectum immediately as the first action
opts.rat: Rectal stimulation (touch, manipulation) is itself a noxious stimulus that WORSENS AD. If bowel is the trigger, anesthetic lidocaine jelly is applied to the rectum first (5-10 min wait), THEN gentle disimpaction. Bladder addressed first regardless.

opts.text: Initiate IV fluid bolus to support cerebral perfusion during the hypertensive episode
opts.rat: Fluids worsen HTN and have no role in AD treatment. The problem is excess vasoconstriction, not volume depletion.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Sit patient up in high Fowler's, loosen constrictive clothing, recheck BP
opts.rat: FIRST - position changes can drop BP within 1-2 minutes. Free, no equipment needed, can be done while preparing the next steps.

opts.text: Insert Foley with lidocaine jelly and drain bladder (740 mL likely the primary trigger); document amount; recheck BP after drainage
opts.rat: SECOND - addresses the most likely trigger. Bladder emptying frequently resolves AD entirely without further intervention.

opts.text: If BP remains >150 SBP after sit-up + bladder drained, apply nitroglycerin paste 1 inch to chest skin above lesion (or give nifedipine IR per orders); reassess BP every 2-5 minutes
opts.rat: THIRD - pharmacologic backup for persistent HTN. Use the most reversible agent first (paste can be wiped off). Continuous BP monitoring guides further dosing.

opts.text: Once acute episode resolves, perform comprehensive search for additional triggers: fecal impaction (with anesthetic prep), full skin assessment, any new procedure or device, restrictive clothing, ingrown toenails
opts.rat: FOURTH - secondary trigger identification. Even after bladder is empty, other triggers may sustain AD. Multiple triggers can coexist and need to be systematically ruled out.

opts.text: Document the episode (trigger, BP trend, interventions, response); educate patient and family on AD recognition (severe headache + sweating above injury = call provider, sit up, find trigger); update care plan to prevent recurrence (cath schedule reinforcement, bowel program review, AD wallet card)
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - prevention and education. SCI patients with AD should carry a wallet card with their condition and treatment protocol because emergency providers unfamiliar with SCI often mistreat them. Patient/family education prevents fatal home episodes.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After bladder drainage, position changes, and supportive care, classify each finding over the next 30 minutes.

opts.text: BP dropped from 198/118 to 124/74 within 15 min of bladder drainage; HR returned to 70; headache resolved; flushing and sweating gone
opts.rat: Textbook response to addressing the trigger - BP often normalizes rapidly once the noxious stimulus is removed. The diagnosis is confirmed retroactively by this response.

opts.text: Patient now alert, no longer anxious, vision clear, able to converse normally about what just happened
opts.rat: Mental status and sensory recovery confirm cerebral perfusion has normalized. AD episodes can be terrifying for patients - debrief and emotional support are part of recovery.

opts.text: Foley drained 740 mL of urine; bladder scan now shows <50 mL residual; ordering provider initiating ciprofloxacin for the UTI
opts.rat: Trigger fully addressed; UTI treatment will reduce future AD risk. Plan to reinforce or restart self-cath schedule with patient and rehab team.

opts.text: BP rebounded to 188/110 after 20 min despite bladder drained; patient developing renewed headache and sweating; manual rectal exam reveals large impacted stool
opts.rat: Secondary trigger uncovered (fecal impaction). Pre-treat with topical lidocaine jelly, wait 5-10 min, then gently disimpact. Common pattern - one trigger resolves and another emerges. The systematic search prevents this from being missed.

opts.text: Sudden onset of left-sided weakness in the upper extremities (above level of injury) and slurred speech
opts.rat: Possible HEMORRHAGIC STROKE from the hypertensive crisis - feared complication of AD. Stat CT head, neurology consult, prepare for ICU transfer. Mortality from AD-related stroke is real.

opts.text: BP now 88/52 with HR 62; patient lightheaded when sitting up
opts.rat: Overshoot from antihypertensive medication or rapid bladder decompression - reverse position (lower the head), administer IV fluid carefully, hold further antihypertensives. Hypotension is the price of treating too aggressively.

opts.text: Patient and wife both verbalize the AD trigger, the warning signs (headache, sweating above injury), the action plan (sit up, find trigger, call provider), and accept an AD wallet card
opts.rat: Education is the most important nursing-led outcome for prevention. SCI patients living in the community must self-recognize AD - medical providers in non-SCI settings often miss it. Wallet cards save lives.

opts.text: Skin below T4 still cool but normal capillary refill; sensation absent below T4 (baseline); normal motor exam below baseline (intact)
opts.rat: Baseline SCI findings - the spinal cord injury itself doesn't change with AD episode resolution. Important to document baseline so any future neurologic decline is detected.

title: Severe Acute Pancreatitis with SIRS
subtitle: 47 y/o male, alcoholic pancreatitis with shock and respiratory compromise
nursesNote: 0830 - 47 y/o male admitted yesterday with severe constant epigastric pain radiating to back, persistent vomiting, and reported daily heavy alcohol use. Initial diagnosis: acute alcoholic pancreatitis. Lipase 2,840 on admission; BISAP score 4 (severe risk). Overnight: increasing dyspnea (RR 28-32), worsening tachycardia (HR 124-138), BP trending down (98/62), oliguria (UO 22 mL/hr last 4 hours), and increasing abdominal distention with rigidity. Skin pale and diaphoretic. Patient now confused and combative compared to last shift, refusing the NG tube the night nurse placed. Bibasilar crackles audible. Abdomen distended, rigid, exquisitely tender; bowel sounds absent; ecchymosis around umbilicus (Cullen's sign) and along left flank (Grey-Turner's sign) noted on morning assessment. ICU bed assigned; fluid resuscitation aggressive (3 L LR overnight); central line being placed; arterial line ordered.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish severe acute pancreatitis with SIRS / impending organ failure.

opts.text: BISAP criteria met (BUN >25, AMS, SIRS, age >60 partial, pleural effusion likely with bibasilar crackles) plus rising lactate 4.6 - all predict severe disease
opts.rat: BISAP (BUN, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age, Pleural effusion) is the validated bedside severity score. Score >=3 = severe pancreatitis with mortality 5-22%. Lactate >4 confirms tissue hypoperfusion - the inflection point from SIRS to multi-organ failure (American College of Gastroenterology 2024).

opts.text: Cullen's sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) and Grey-Turner's sign (flank ecchymosis) indicate retroperitoneal bleeding and severe necrotizing pancreatitis
opts.rat: Both signs are LATE findings (24-72 hr) reflecting hemorrhagic/necrotizing pancreatitis - associated with mortality up to 37%. Pancreatic enzymes have escaped the gland and digested surrounding tissue. Diagnostic of severe disease.

opts.text: Distributive shock physiology emerging: HR 138, BP 92/58, RR 32, SpO2 88%, Hct 52% (hemoconcentration), oliguria - classic third-spacing with intravascular depletion despite 3 L fluid
opts.rat: Massive third-spacing into the retroperitoneum and abdomen. The 'pancreatitis is dehydrating' principle - patients can need 6-10 L in the first 24 hours. Hct 52% (hemoconcentration without bleeding) and oliguria confirm intravascular depletion despite generous fluids. SpO2 88% suggests early ARDS.

opts.text: Lipase 3,640 U/L
opts.rat: Confirms the DIAGNOSIS of pancreatitis but does NOT correlate with severity. Lipase >3x upper limit is diagnostic; the absolute number doesn't predict outcome. A patient with mild pancreatitis can have lipase 5,000.

opts.text: Glucose 286 mg/dL
opts.rat: Hyperglycemia is common in pancreatitis (transient or new diabetes from islet cell injury). Important to manage but not a severity cue specifically.

opts.text: Reported heavy alcohol use
opts.rat: ETIOLOGY of the pancreatitis (alcohol) - important to know for treatment of underlying cause but does not establish the current severity.

opts.text: Calcium 7.2 with magnesium 1.4
opts.rat: Hypocalcemia is COMMON in pancreatitis (calcium saponification with fat necrosis) and is a Ranson criterion. Worth correcting and monitoring for tetany/QT prolongation, but not by itself the most severe cue.

opts.text: Refusing the NG tube placed by night nurse
opts.rat: Important nursing/care issue but not a clinical cue establishing severity. NG tube use in pancreatitis is reserved for ileus/obstruction with vomiting - not all patients need it.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Pancreatic enzymes (trypsin, lipase, elastase) activate prematurely WITHIN the pancreas instead of the duodenum, causing autodigestion - the gland is digesting itself; spillover into the bloodstream causes systemic inflammation, capillary leak, and multi-organ injury
opts.rat: Core mechanism of acute pancreatitis. The enzymes that should digest food in the duodenum activate too early and digest the pancreas + surrounding fat + retroperitoneum. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 1089.

opts.text: Hypocalcemia in pancreatitis results from calcium saponification - calcium binds to free fatty acids released by lipase digestion of fat, forming chalky white deposits visible on imaging; severe hypocalcemia (Chvostek's, Trousseau's signs) requires IV calcium replacement
opts.rat: Distinctive pancreatitis-specific mechanism. Saponification means calcium is consumed by the inflammatory process. Severe hypocalcemia can prolong QT, cause tetany, and increase mortality. Replace IV when symptomatic or <7.5.

opts.text: Aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (typically Lactated Ringer's at 5-10 mL/kg/hr or 250-500 mL/hr) within the first 24 hours is the most important intervention to prevent pancreatic necrosis and reduce mortality - target UO >0.5 mL/kg/hr
opts.rat: Goal-directed fluid resuscitation is class IA. LR is preferred over NS (NS causes hyperchloremic acidosis and may worsen inflammation per the WATERFALL trial). Inadequate resuscitation is the #1 modifiable mortality factor in early pancreatitis.

opts.text: Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics should be given to all severe pancreatitis patients to prevent infected necrosis
opts.rat: OUTDATED practice. Prophylactic antibiotics in non-infected pancreatitis do NOT improve outcomes and may increase resistant infections (multiple meta-analyses). Antibiotics ONLY for confirmed infected necrosis or extra-pancreatic infection. Common NCLEX trap.

opts.text: Patients with severe pancreatitis should be made strictly NPO with TPN until lipase normalizes to prevent stimulating the pancreas
opts.rat: OUTDATED. Early ENTERAL nutrition (within 24-72 hours, often via NJ or NG tube past the pylorus) IMPROVES outcomes vs TPN - reduces infections, ICU length of stay, mortality. NPO + TPN is no longer standard. Major guideline shift.

opts.text: Morphine should be avoided in pancreatitis because it causes Sphincter of Oddi spasm and worsens the disease
opts.rat: Largely DEBUNKED myth. The theoretical Sphincter of Oddi effect has not been shown clinically significant. Patient comfort with adequate analgesia is essential. Hydromorphone and fentanyl are commonly used; morphine is not contraindicated. Old NCLEX teaching that's been updated.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the most likely complications/contributors to deterioration from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: SIRS progressing to distributive shock from massive third-spacing and inflammatory cascade
opts.rat: Most likely current pathophysiology - explains the BP drop, tachycardia, oliguria, and hemoconcentration despite fluids. Treatment is more aggressive fluid resuscitation.

opts.text: Early ARDS from inflammatory pulmonary capillary leak (pancreatitis-associated lung injury)
opts.rat: RR 32, SpO2 88%, bibasilar crackles - early ARDS picture. Pancreatitis is a leading non-pulmonary cause of ARDS via systemic inflammation. Will likely require mechanical ventilation.

opts.text: Pancreatic necrosis (sterile or infected) developing
opts.rat: Cullen's and Grey-Turner's signs suggest necrotizing process. Sterile necrosis develops in first 7-10 days; infection of necrotic tissue typically follows. CT with contrast at 72-96 hours assesses extent.

opts.text: Abdominal compartment syndrome from massive retroperitoneal edema and ascites
opts.rat: Rigid, distended abdomen with oliguria and rising peak airway pressures suggests this. Bladder pressure measurement diagnoses; if >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction, decompressive intervention may be needed.

opts.text: Acute kidney injury (pre-renal from hypoperfusion progressing to ATN)
opts.rat: Oliguria with rising BUN suggests pre-renal AKI. May progress to ATN if hypoperfusion sustained. Aggressive fluid resuscitation and avoidance of nephrotoxins are the treatment.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next hour.

opts.text: Aggressive Lactated Ringer's resuscitation at 5-10 mL/kg/hr (e.g., 500 mL/hr) with reassessment q4h targeting UO >0.5 mL/kg/hr, HR <120, MAP >65, lactate clearance, and stable hematocrit
opts.rat: First-line treatment that determines mortality. Pre-determined goal-directed protocol with frequent reassessment. WATERFALL trial showed moderate (vs aggressive) resuscitation reduces fluid overload while still meeting targets - tailor to response.

opts.text: Replace electrolytes: IV calcium gluconate for symptomatic hypocalcemia, IV magnesium 2-4 g, monitor for tetany/QT prolongation; manage hyperglycemia with insulin if persistent >180
opts.rat: Essential supportive care. Hypocalcemia and hypomagnesemia together can cause refractory hypotension, arrhythmias, and tetany. Hyperglycemia is harmful at sustained >180 - usually managed with continuous insulin in ICU.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU transfer; arterial line, central line for fluids/pressors; prepare for mechanical ventilation given progressive respiratory compromise; obtain CT with contrast at 72-96 hr to assess for necrosis
opts.rat: Severe pancreatitis with shock and respiratory compromise needs ICU. CT with contrast diagnoses necrosis but is timed at 72-96 hr (earlier scans miss necrosis still developing). Anticipatory readiness for intubation - early ARDS often requires mechanical support.

opts.text: Initiate prophylactic IV piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn)
opts.rat: Prophylactic antibiotics in sterile pancreatitis do NOT improve outcomes per current guidelines. Use only for documented infection or infected necrosis (FNA-confirmed or strong clinical suspicion).

opts.text: Place an NG tube on continuous suction; keep NPO with TPN for 7-10 days
opts.rat: Outdated approach. Early enteral nutrition (within 72 hr) via NJ tube past the pylorus is preferred when tolerated. NG suction reserved for ileus with persistent vomiting. TPN is associated with worse outcomes vs enteral feeding in pancreatitis.

opts.text: Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV bolus to provide caloric support
opts.rat: Patient is already hyperglycemic at 286. D50 bolus would worsen hyperglycemia, increase metabolic stress, and provide negligible nutrition. Wrong intervention.

opts.text: Administer IV nicardipine to lower BP and reduce cardiac workload
opts.rat: Patient is HYPOTENSIVE (92/58). Nicardipine would precipitate cardiovascular collapse. Treat the shock with fluids and pressors if needed, not antihypertensives.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitoring with high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather; ensure two large-bore IVs patent; sit patient up if tolerated for respiratory distress; prepare BVM and intubation kit at bedside
opts.rat: FIRST - ABCs. Respiratory compromise (RR 32, SpO2 88%) plus shock plus AMS = high risk for sudden deterioration. Equipment readiness is anticipatory.

opts.text: Bolus 1-2 L LR rapidly while ordering ongoing infusion at 250-500 mL/hr; recheck VS, mental status, UO, and lactate every 30-60 min for response; insert Foley if not present
opts.rat: SECOND - definitive resuscitation. Lactated Ringer's bolus addresses the third-spacing immediately. Foley enables hourly UO tracking - the bedside marker of adequacy.

opts.text: Administer IV calcium gluconate, IV magnesium, manage glucose with insulin per protocol; address pain with IV opioid (fentanyl preferred over morphine in shock); antiemetic for nausea/vomiting
opts.rat: THIRD - supportive care. Electrolyte replacement prevents complications; pain control is humane and reduces sympathetic overdrive; antiemetic reduces aspiration risk and respiratory work.

opts.text: Coordinate with ICU intensivist for transfer; arrange central line and arterial line; SBAR to receiving team; consider gastroenterology consult for endoscopic evaluation if biliary obstruction suspected
opts.rat: FOURTH - escalation of care and team coordination. ICU level monitoring is required. GI involvement helps with nutrition planning and rules out biliary obstruction (gallstone pancreatitis would warrant ERCP).

opts.text: Update family on severity, treatment plan, expected ICU course, prognosis, and need for ongoing alcohol cessation support; offer chaplaincy and social work; document advance directive review
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - critical communication and goals-of-care alignment. Severe pancreatitis carries 20-30% mortality; family needs realistic expectations. Alcohol cessation support is part of long-term recovery.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After ICU transfer, fluid resuscitation, electrolyte replacement, and supportive care, classify each finding over the next 24-48 hours.

opts.text: MAP 72 mmHg off vasopressors; HR 92; UO sustained at 60 mL/hr; lactate cleared from 4.6 to 1.4 mmol/L; mental status returned to alert and oriented
opts.rat: Successful resuscitation - all four organ-dysfunction markers (hemodynamic, renal, perfusion, neurologic) trending toward normal. Begin tapering fluid rate; transition to maintenance.

opts.text: Hematocrit dropped from 52% to 38%, calcium normalized to 8.6, magnesium 2.0; tolerating NJ enteral feeds at goal rate without nausea or pain increase
opts.rat: Hemoconcentration resolution confirms intravascular volume restoration. Successful early enteral nutrition is associated with significantly better outcomes vs TPN. Important treatment milestone.

opts.text: Repeat CT with contrast at 72 hours: walled-off necrosis 30% of pancreas, no infection markers, abdomen no longer rigid, bowel sounds returned
opts.rat: Sterile necrosis can be managed conservatively if patient stable. Abdominal exam improvement and return of bowel sounds suggest resolution of ileus. Continue close monitoring for infection of necrosis (typically 2-4 weeks out).

opts.text: Sudden new fever 39.4°C with rising WBC 28K, dropping BP, and CT showing gas within necrotic pancreas at day 14
opts.rat: INFECTED NECROSIS - the most feared late complication. Gas in necrosis is pathognomonic. Mortality exceeds 30%. Treatment: IV antibiotics with pancreatic penetration (carbapenem), step-up approach (drain first, then minimally invasive necrosectomy if needed). Open surgery is last resort.

opts.text: Worsening hypoxemia: PaO2/FiO2 ratio drops below 200; bilateral infiltrates on CXR; requires intubation with high PEEP
opts.rat: Pancreatitis-associated ARDS. Lung-protective ventilation (TV 6 mL/kg, plateau <30, permissive hypercapnia), prone positioning if severe. Common cause of mortality in severe pancreatitis.

opts.text: New sustained tachycardia, refractory hypotension despite norepinephrine, oliguria, rising peak airway pressures, intra-abdominal pressure 26 mmHg
opts.rat: Abdominal compartment syndrome. Decompressive intervention indicated - paracentesis if ascites driving pressure, decompressive laparotomy if necessary. Mortality if untreated is high.

opts.text: Patient verbalizing intent to pursue alcohol cessation; accepted referrals to addiction medicine and outpatient counseling; family engaged in education about pancreatitis recurrence and dietary changes
opts.rat: Critical psychosocial outcomes. Alcoholic pancreatitis recurs in 50% with continued drinking; cessation reduces recurrence to ~20%. Hospitalization is a 'reachable moment.' Concrete referrals during admission improve long-term sobriety substantially.

opts.text: Persistent low-grade epigastric pain controlled with PRN oral hydromorphone; mild nausea with eating; trending downward as healing progresses
opts.rat: Expected recovery course. Pancreatitis pain often persists for weeks as inflammation resolves. As long as severity is decreasing and not worsening, this is part of normal recovery. Educate patient on realistic timeline.

title: Thyroid Storm (Thyrotoxic Crisis)
subtitle: 44 y/o female, untreated Graves disease, post-op crisis after emergency appendectomy
nursesNote: 0445 - 44 y/o female, post-op day 1 from emergency laparoscopic appendectomy. Pre-op history significant for 8-month complaint of palpitations, weight loss (22 lbs), heat intolerance, anxiety, tremor; never fully evaluated due to lack of insurance and was not on any thyroid therapy at admission. Post-op course initially uncomplicated. Over the past 4 hours, patient developed escalating tachycardia (HR 152 -> 168 -> 184), high fever (39.6 C / 103.3 F), profuse sweating, agitation alternating with confusion, severe nausea/vomiting, and now diarrhea. Skin warm, flushed, and drenched. Hand tremor severe. Bilateral exophthalmos and an enlarged thyroid noted on exam. Patient was given acetaminophen 650 mg PR and an aspirin 325 mg PO 30 min ago by a covering provider for the fever before this nurse came on shift. New onset atrial fibrillation with RVR on the monitor. Endocrinology consulted; ICU bed pending; ABG drawn; thyroid panel drawn (results pending - prior outpatient labs from clinic visit showed undetectable TSH, T4 24, free T4 7.8).

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish thyroid storm rather than other causes of post-op tachycardia/fever.

opts.text: The hyperthermia-tachycardia-AMS triad with progressive escalation despite acetaminophen: temp 39.6 C, HR 184, agitation alternating with confusion in a patient with known untreated Graves disease
opts.rat: The Burch-Wartofsky scoring system identifies thyroid storm by combining thermoregulatory dysfunction, CNS effects, GI/hepatic dysfunction, and cardiovascular dysfunction. Score >=45 = highly suggestive. This patient is well over that threshold. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 1207.

opts.text: Recent surgery (laparoscopic appendectomy) as a known precipitant in a patient with undertreated/untreated hyperthyroidism
opts.rat: Surgery, infection, trauma, DKA, MI, parturition, and iodine load are the classic thyroid storm triggers. The combination of underlying hyperthyroidism + acute stressor is the recipe. Surgery on an undertreated thyrotoxic patient is high-risk and often the defining trigger.

opts.text: Pre-existing physical signs (exophthalmos, goiter, weight loss, tremor) plus undetectable TSH with T4 nearly twice normal
opts.rat: Confirms underlying Graves disease - the substrate for storm. Outpatient labs locked in the diagnosis even before storm physiology developed. Exophthalmos is specific to Graves; goiter + tremor confirm a hyperthyroid state.

opts.text: New atrial fibrillation with RVR
opts.rat: A FREQUENT consequence of thyroid storm (high-output cardiac state) but not unique to storm - many post-op patients develop AFib. Reflects severity but doesn't establish the diagnosis.

opts.text: Calcium 11.4 mg/dL
opts.rat: Hypercalcemia is COMMON in thyroid storm (hyperthyroidism increases bone turnover and calcium release) but is a supporting lab, not a primary diagnostic cue.

opts.text: Glucose 186 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia from catecholamine surge - present in many critical illnesses. Not specific.

opts.text: Lactate 3.4 mmol/L
opts.rat: Reflects tissue hypoperfusion from high-output state and dehydration - severity marker, not a specific diagnostic cue.

opts.text: Recent post-op aspirin and acetaminophen administration
opts.rat: The aspirin is a CONCERN (worsens thyroid storm by displacing T4 from binding proteins) but documents an iatrogenic exposure rather than a diagnostic cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: Thyroid storm reflects an exaggerated catecholamine response to thyroid hormone excess - excessive beta-adrenergic stimulation causes the tachycardia, fever, tremor, and cardiac strain; beta-blockade (typically propranolol) blunts this and is FIRST-LINE
opts.rat: Catecholamine sensitization is the core mechanism. Propranolol is preferred because it also inhibits T4-to-T3 peripheral conversion. Reduces HR, tremor, agitation, and cardiac demand. Should be given even before antithyroid drugs.

opts.text: Antithyroid medications (PTU or methimazole) block new thyroid hormone synthesis but do NOT lower already-circulating hormone - they take days to weeks to fully work; PTU is preferred in storm because it also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion
opts.rat: Critical concept. PTU and methimazole are slow. PTU is first-line in storm specifically because of the conversion-blocking effect. Methimazole is preferred for chronic hyperthyroidism (better side effect profile, once-daily dosing) but PTU wins in acute crisis.

opts.text: Iodine (Lugol's solution or potassium iodide / SSKI) blocks thyroid hormone RELEASE from the gland and must be given AT LEAST 1 HOUR AFTER the antithyroid drug - if given first, the iodine becomes substrate for new hormone synthesis (Wolff-Chaikoff escape) and worsens the storm
opts.rat: The Wolff-Chaikoff/Jod-Basedow paradox. Sequence matters: Beta-blocker first, PTU second, iodine third (1 hour later). NCLEX-tested concept. Reverses the gland's storage and release pattern.

opts.text: Aspirin (salicylates) is the ideal antipyretic in thyroid storm because it works rapidly and reduces inflammation
opts.rat: DANGEROUS and SPECIFICALLY CONTRAINDICATED. Salicylates DISPLACE thyroid hormone from binding proteins (TBG), increasing free hormone and worsening the storm. Acetaminophen is the antipyretic of choice. The aspirin this patient received needs to be flagged in the chart and avoided going forward.

opts.text: Cooling measures should aim to rapidly drop core temperature below 37 C using ice baths and ice packs to all major arteries
opts.rat: AGGRESSIVE cooling causes shivering which INCREASES metabolic demand and worsens the storm. Use tepid sponging, cooling blankets, and acetaminophen. If shivering occurs, sedation may be needed. Goal is gradual reduction, not rapid drop.

opts.text: Calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are first-line for the rate control of thyroid-storm-related atrial fibrillation
opts.rat: Beta-blockers are FIRST-LINE for both the storm itself AND rate control. CCBs are second-line if beta-blockers contraindicated (severe asthma) and have less impact on thyroid hormone metabolism.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Thyroid storm precipitated by surgery in patient with untreated Graves disease
opts.rat: Burch-Wartofsky-positive presentation with classic precipitant (surgery), confirmatory outpatient labs, and characteristic exam findings. Diagnostic.

opts.text: Sepsis from post-op infection (unrecognized peritonitis or other source)
opts.rat: Always must be ruled out - shares the fever-tachycardia-AMS picture. Cultures, surgical site exam, and CT imaging may be appropriate. Often coexists with storm and may be the trigger itself.

opts.text: Malignant hyperthermia (MH) from anesthesia exposure
opts.rat: Hyperthermia + tachycardia + AMS post-op raises MH on the differential. Classic MH features (muscle rigidity, hypercapnia, mixed acidosis with massive CK rise) less prominent here. Important to consider, especially in undiagnosed thyroid storm.

opts.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome / serotonin syndrome
opts.rat: No relevant medication exposure documented. Lower on the list but always part of the hyperthermia differential.

opts.text: Pheochromocytoma crisis
opts.rat: Catecholamine surge fits the picture, but no history of episodic spells, classic headache-palpitations-diaphoresis triad. Less likely but on the differential for refractory cases.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next hour.

opts.text: Administer IV propranolol 1-2 mg every 10-15 min (or oral 60-80 mg q4h via NG if oral route possible) to control heart rate and blunt catecholamine response
opts.rat: FIRST-LINE. Reduces HR, BP, tremor, agitation, and inhibits T4-to-T3 conversion. Esmolol drip is alternative for fine titration. Hold if heart failure decompensation - patient is at risk for tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy already.

opts.text: Administer PTU 500-1000 mg loading dose orally/NG, then 250 mg q4h (or methimazole 60-80 mg loading then divided dosing); withhold iodine for at least 1 hour AFTER PTU
opts.rat: PTU blocks new hormone synthesis AND peripheral conversion. The 1-hour delay before iodine prevents the Wolff-Chaikoff escape phenomenon. Sequence matters.

opts.text: Treat hyperthermia with acetaminophen (NOT aspirin), tepid sponging, cooling blankets; aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (insensible losses are massive); administer IV hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h for relative adrenal insufficiency
opts.rat: Multiple stress points addressed. Hydrocortisone is class I recommendation in storm - addresses relative adrenal insufficiency from hypermetabolic state and inhibits T4-to-T3 conversion. Acetaminophen NOT aspirin (the previously-administered aspirin needs to be on the chart's avoid list).

opts.text: Administer aspirin 650 mg PR for fever control as the most rapid antipyretic
opts.rat: EXPLICITLY CONTRAINDICATED in thyroid storm. Salicylates displace T4 from binding proteins, increasing free hormone and worsening storm. NCLEX favorite trap.

opts.text: Begin levothyroxine 100 mcg IV daily to address the underlying thyroid dysfunction
opts.rat: Levothyroxine is treatment for HYPOthyroidism, not hyperthyroidism. Giving thyroid hormone in storm is potentially fatal. Distractor that tests whether students confuse hypo vs hyper management.

opts.text: Apply ice packs to major vessels (groin, axilla, neck) and immerse hands in ice water
opts.rat: Aggressive cooling triggers shivering which worsens hypermetabolic state. Use cooling blanket and acetaminophen for gradual temperature reduction. Sedation if shivering develops.

opts.text: Administer iodine (SSKI 5 drops PO) immediately upon arrival to suppress thyroid hormone release
opts.rat: WRONG ORDER. Iodine before PTU paradoxically increases hormone synthesis (provides substrate). Always: beta-blocker -> PTU -> wait at least 1 hour -> THEN iodine. Sequence is exam-tested.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac monitor; administer high-flow oxygen; ensure two large-bore IVs; prepare crash cart and vasopressors at bedside given AFib RVR and possible cardiac decompensation
opts.rat: FIRST - cardiovascular safety. Hypermetabolic state plus AFib RVR puts patient at risk for cardiogenic shock or arrhythmia. Equipment readiness is anticipatory.

opts.text: Administer IV propranolol 1 mg slow IV push; reassess HR and BP every 5-10 min; titrate as needed
opts.rat: SECOND - immediate beta-blockade addresses tachycardia, tremor, and catecholamine surge. The intervention with the most rapid hemodynamic effect.

opts.text: Administer PTU 500-1000 mg orally/NG loading dose; document time given (the 1-hour clock for iodine starts here); then administer Lugol's solution or SSKI after at least 1 hour delay
opts.rat: THIRD - definitive medical therapy with the correct timing sequence. Document time of PTU prominently so the next nurse knows when iodine becomes safe.

opts.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 100 mg; begin aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (target UO 0.5 mL/kg/hr); cool with acetaminophen and cooling blanket - flag aspirin as contraindicated in chart and avoid list
opts.rat: FOURTH - supportive measures and prevention of further harm. Steroids address adrenal insufficiency and conversion. Fluids replace massive insensible losses. Chart correction prevents repeat aspirin error.

opts.text: Coordinate ICU transfer with bed control, SBAR to receiving team and endocrinology, update family on diagnosis, severity, and treatment plan; address need for definitive therapy (radioactive iodine ablation or thyroidectomy) once storm resolved
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - escalation and family communication. Recovery from storm takes days; planning for definitive treatment of underlying Graves disease is part of preventing recurrence.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After ICU transfer with beta-blockade, antithyroid drugs, hydrocortisone, and supportive care, classify each finding over the next 24-48 hours.

opts.text: HR decreased from 184 to 96 in normal sinus rhythm; temp 37.8 C; agitation resolved; conversational and oriented x3
opts.rat: Successful blunting of catecholamine response with beta-blockade plus reduction in thyroid hormone effects. The triad of fever-tachycardia-AMS resolving is the strongest improvement marker.

opts.text: Free T4 trending from 7.8 to 4.2 ng/dL; TSH still suppressed; calcium normalized to 9.4
opts.rat: Antithyroid drugs and iodine working. TSH takes weeks to months to normalize - lagging indicator. Calcium normalization reflects reduced bone turnover as hyperthyroid state diminishes.

opts.text: Tolerating oral PTU with normal bowel sounds, returned to normal post-op diet, surgical site clean, AFib resolved to sinus rhythm with continued propranolol
opts.rat: Successful storm resolution with restored GI function and normal post-op recovery proceeding. Continue PTU until definitive therapy planned.

opts.text: Sudden return of HR 168, temp 39.2 C, severe agitation, jaundice, RUQ tenderness; AST 480, ALT 320
opts.rat: Two concurrent problems - storm relapse AND acute hepatotoxicity (likely PTU-related). PTU has black-box warning for fulminant hepatic failure. Switch to methimazole, escalate care, hepatology consult. Storm-relapse possibilities include incomplete therapy or new precipitant.

opts.text: New-onset crackles to mid-lung fields, dyspnea, S3 gallop, BNP 1820, elevated jugular venous distension
opts.rat: High-output heart failure - thyrotoxic cardiomyopathy. Beta-blocker can worsen this acutely. Add diuretics, may need to reduce beta-blocker dose, ICU close monitoring. Common cause of mortality in storm.

opts.text: Patient stating 'I can't believe I let this go so long' and asking about radioactive iodine ablation; accepting referral to outpatient endocrinology for definitive therapy
opts.rat: Critical psychosocial outcome. Storm survival is meaningless if underlying Graves goes untreated - recurrence risk is high. Definitive therapy (RAI or thyroidectomy) prevents future storms. Patient engagement signals readiness.

opts.text: Mild proximal muscle weakness and persistent fine tremor; lab values trending down but still elevated; appetite returning
opts.rat: Expected slow recovery course. Thyroid hormone half-life is 7-10 days; symptoms persist for weeks even with treatment. As long as values are trending down, this is normal recovery, not deterioration.

opts.text: Aspirin documented as ALLERGIC/AVOID in EHR with note 'displaces thyroid hormone - contraindicated in hyperthyroidism/storm'; patient and family educated on this to prevent future iatrogenic harm
opts.rat: Critical safety outcome. The aspirin error must not recur. Chart-level prevention plus patient/family education reduces risk in future emergency presentations where providers may not know the patient's history.

title: Status Epilepticus
subtitle: 38 y/o male, AED nonadherent epilepsy, seizing for 25+ minutes at home
nursesNote: 0312 - 38 y/o male with history of epilepsy (focal-onset with secondary generalization, dx age 19 after closed head injury) on levetiracetam 1500 mg BID. Wife reports patient ran out of medication 4 days ago - has been waiting on prior auth from new insurance. Witnessed by wife to have generalized tonic-clonic seizure at approximately 0245; called EMS at 0250 when seizure did not stop. EMS arrival 0258 - patient still convulsing; received midazolam 5 mg IM en route. Arrived ED 0307 with continued generalized convulsive activity (eyes rolled back, rhythmic 4-extremity clonic movements, frothing at mouth, urinary incontinence, no purposeful response). Total seizure time approaching 30 minutes. Bilateral tongue laceration noted; abrasion to left forehead from ground impact. IV established en route; second IV being placed; airway managed with NPA and BVM with high-flow O2. Glucose check 92. Bedside EEG ordered, neuro and ICU paged.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish convulsive status epilepticus requiring immediate aggressive treatment.

opts.text: Continuous generalized convulsive activity for approximately 30 minutes without return to baseline - far exceeds the 5-minute operational threshold defining status epilepticus
opts.rat: Modern definition (ILAE 2015): generalized convulsive seizure lasting >5 minutes OR two or more seizures without full recovery between them = status epilepticus. The old 30-minute definition is obsolete; treatment starts at 5 minutes because neuronal injury begins around then. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 1366.

opts.text: Compensation failure: SpO2 89% despite BVM, lactate 8.4, pH 7.18, PaCO2 58 - tissue hypoxia and severe mixed acidosis from sustained muscular activity and inadequate ventilation
opts.rat: After 30 minutes of continuous seizing, the patient has decompensated. Lactic acidosis from anaerobic muscle metabolism plus respiratory acidosis from inadequate ventilation. This is the inflection point where neurons start to die from energy failure - 'time is brain.'

opts.text: Levetiracetam level <2 mcg/mL (subtherapeutic) in a known epileptic patient who ran out of medication 4 days ago - establishes the precipitant
opts.rat: AED nonadherence is the #1 cause of breakthrough seizures in known epileptics. Subtherapeutic levels confirm the trigger and shape the management plan (must restart and bridge therapy).

opts.text: Tongue laceration and forehead abrasion
opts.rat: These are CONSEQUENCES of seizure activity (injury during fall/convulsion) and confirm a true convulsive event, but they don't establish the duration or severity that defines status.

opts.text: Glucose 92 mg/dL
opts.rat: NORMAL glucose - rules OUT hypoglycemia as a cause but is not itself a cue establishing status. Always check glucose in any seizure - hypoglycemia is treatable and missed cases cause permanent injury.

opts.text: CK 1,840 U/L
opts.rat: Elevated CK from sustained muscle contraction warns of impending RHABDOMYOLYSIS but is a complication marker, not a diagnostic cue for status itself.

opts.text: Urinary incontinence during the seizure
opts.rat: Common feature of generalized tonic-clonic seizures (along with tongue biting and post-ictal state) - helps confirm a true seizure but doesn't establish status epilepticus.

opts.text: Wife being available to give a clear seizure history
opts.rat: Critical for diagnosis and rule-out of differentials, but witness availability is not itself a clinical cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Link cues to pathophysiology and treatment
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam IV is first-line) potentiate GABA-A receptors, the major inhibitory neurotransmission system - they break the runaway excitatory loop that defines a seizure; lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (typical adult dose 4 mg) repeated once if needed in 5-10 min
opts.rat: Class IA recommendation per AES 2016 guidelines. Lorazepam is preferred over diazepam because of longer CNS half-life (12 hr vs 20 min) - diazepam redistributes out of brain quickly and seizure can recur. IM midazolam is preferred when no IV access. Goal: stop the seizure within 20 min.

opts.text: Second-line therapy after benzodiazepines: IV fosphenytoin 20 PE/kg, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, or valproate 40 mg/kg - these prevent seizure recurrence and bridge to long-term AED therapy; fosphenytoin is preferred over phenytoin because it can be infused faster and does not cause purple glove syndrome with extravasation
opts.rat: ESETT trial (2019) showed levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate are EQUALLY effective as second-line. Choice depends on contraindications: avoid valproate in liver disease and women of childbearing age; avoid fosphenytoin in advanced heart block. Prepare second-line agent during benzodiazepine administration.

opts.text: Refractory status epilepticus (continued seizure despite first AND second-line agents) requires anesthetic doses of propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital infusion with intubation and continuous EEG monitoring - target burst suppression on EEG
opts.rat: Refractory SE develops in 30-40% of patients. Requires ICU and continuous EEG. Anesthetic infusions for 24-48 hr followed by gradual wean. Mortality of refractory SE is 15-30%, super-refractory (>24 hr on infusion) up to 50%.

opts.text: Phenytoin can be safely pushed rapidly (>50 mg/min) through any peripheral IV line for fastest seizure control
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Rapid phenytoin infusion causes hypotension and arrhythmias. Maximum rate 50 mg/min (25 mg/min in elderly/cardiac disease). Extravasation causes 'purple glove syndrome' - severe tissue necrosis. Fosphenytoin (water-soluble prodrug) is far safer.

opts.text: An actively convulsing patient should be physically restrained to prevent injury and forced to bite a padded tongue blade to prevent tongue laceration
opts.rat: OBSOLETE and HARMFUL. Never put anything in the mouth of a seizing patient (aspiration, dental injury, biter injury). Don't restrain limbs forcefully (fracture risk). Move sharp objects away, pad surroundings, position in left lateral decubitus when possible to reduce aspiration.

opts.text: After the seizure stops, the patient will be immediately oriented and able to give a coherent history within 10-15 minutes
opts.rat: Post-ictal state typically lasts 30 min to several hours after generalized seizure. Slow neurologic recovery is EXPECTED and does not by itself indicate continued subclinical seizure. However, if no improvement after 30-60 min, suspect non-convulsive status epilepticus (NCSE) - requires continuous EEG.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank causes of the breakthrough status from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: AED nonadherence / subtherapeutic levetiracetam (ran out 4 days ago, level <2)
opts.rat: Confirmed by history and lab. The single most common cause of breakthrough seizures and status in known epileptics. Restart and bridge plan is the core intervention.

opts.text: Sleep deprivation, concurrent illness, or other lowered seizure threshold superimposed on AED noncompliance
opts.rat: Frequent compounding factor. The wife should be asked about recent illness, sleep, alcohol, stimulants, or new medications that may have lowered threshold further.

opts.text: New structural lesion (acute stroke, tumor progression, intracranial hemorrhage, or progression of old TBI)
opts.rat: Cannot be ruled out without imaging. CT head (then MRI if negative) is mandatory after stabilization. Especially relevant given history of closed head injury at age 19.

opts.text: Metabolic derangement (severe hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, uremia, hypoglycemia)
opts.rat: Lower likelihood given normal glucose, normal sodium, and normal creatinine on initial labs - but full chem panel and Mg/Ca should be checked.

opts.text: CNS infection (meningitis, encephalitis)
opts.rat: Less likely given no fever, headache, or meningeal signs in history - but if no clear cause emerges and clinical suspicion warrants, LP after imaging should be considered.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 10-20 minutes.

opts.text: Administer IV lorazepam 4 mg (0.1 mg/kg) now; if seizure continues at 5 min, repeat lorazepam 4 mg once; document times of each dose precisely - the clock drives every escalation decision
opts.rat: First-line per AES algorithm. Time-stamped dosing is essential because the next escalation depends on time elapsed. If no IV: midazolam 10 mg IM (or buccal/intranasal). Diazepam PR 20 mg is an option but inferior.

opts.text: Position to optimize airway: head-of-bed elevated 30 degrees if not actively convulsing, or left lateral decubitus during convulsive activity to reduce aspiration; suction PRN; continue BVM with 100% O2; prepare RSI equipment at bedside (intubation likely if seizure continues or after second-line agent)
opts.rat: ABCs always. Cannot intubate during active convulsions reliably; protect airway with positioning and BVM until seizure breaks. Most patients with refractory SE end up intubated for the propofol/midazolam infusion that follows. Anticipatory equipment readiness.

opts.text: Begin loading dose of second-line AED in parallel: fosphenytoin 20 PE/kg IV (max rate 150 PE/min) OR levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV - have it ready to push if benzodiazepine fails to break seizure within 5-10 min
opts.rat: Don't wait until benzo fails to start preparing. Pharmacy should be working on the second-line agent during the first benzo dose. Time to second-line agent is a major outcome driver - aim for <20 min from arrival.

opts.text: Administer IV dextrose 50% (D50) 50 mL bolus empirically given the seizure activity
opts.rat: Glucose is NORMAL at 92. D50 in a non-hypoglycemic patient is unnecessary and may worsen Wernicke encephalopathy in alcoholic patients (give thiamine first if alcoholic). The protocol is: CHECK glucose first, treat only if low.
